vlog.Problematic: [IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-0.jpg[/IMAGE]Industrial production lines, medical laboratories, and precision handling environments increasingly rely on robotic manipulators to execute repeatable, high-accuracy tasks under strict safety and productivity constraints.[IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-1.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 1: Production Line Robotic Arms[/CAPTION] Human-only workflows struggle with fatigue, repeatability, and hazardous conditions, while commercial robotic systems are often expensive and closed for experimentation. To address this gap, the Mediterranean Institute of Technology developed the MEDTECH ERA 6-axis robotic arm as a demonstrator platform. While the arm itself was capable, the real challenge lay in delivering a safe, maintainable, and standards-compliant electrical and control infrastructure suitable for repeated demonstrations and multi-event operation.
vlog.Features: The MEDTECH ERA robotic arm development was divided among three teams: mechanical, software, and electrical. I contributed to the electrical team, where my primary responsibility was designing an efficient and safe electrical cabinet.[IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-2.jpg[/IMAGE][IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-3.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 2: Preparing The Cabinet Layout[/CAPTION][CAPTION]Figure 3: The Primary Cabinet Layout[/CAPTION] I planned the component layout to meet safety requirements while maximizing airflow, created detailed schematics in QElectroTech, and designed a microcontroller support PCB in KiCad to replace loose wiring with a connectorized interface.[IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-4.jpg[/IMAGE][IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-5.jpg[/IMAGE][IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-6.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 4: Board Schematics[/CAPTION][CAPTION]Figure 5: Board Layout[/CAPTION][CAPTION]Figure 6: Board 3D View[/CAPTION] During assembly, I implemented organized wire routing through ducts, separated power and control lines, minimized electromagnetic interference, and developed an active cooling strategy to manage heat.[IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-7.jpg[/IMAGE][IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-8.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 7: Modular Distribution Block[/CAPTION][CAPTION]Figure 8: Circuit Breakers[/CAPTION] This approach transformed the system from a bench prototype into a professional-grade, enclosure-ready control platform.
vlog.Challenges: Reference materials often defined system functions but lacked implementation-ready details, requiring me to create missing schematic symbols and PCB footprints from datasheets. Some components were unavailable locally, necessitating electrical equivalence checks and redesigns.[IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-9.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 9: Assembling the components on the steel plate[/CAPTION]Cross-team dependencies required careful coordination of mechanical layouts, controller interfaces, and wiring plans before final assembly. Cabinet layout had to satisfy spacing, accessibility, and airflow constraints while staying within enclosure dimensions.
vlog.Solution: I implemented a control cabinet architecture with separated power and control zones, labeled terminal interfaces, and protected distribution paths. Schematics were created in QElectroTech with normalized references and net labeling. The microcontroller support PCB was designed in KiCad with rule-driven routing and connectorized I/O headers. Wiring was routed through ducts to minimize loop area and electromagnetic coupling, and active ventilation was added to maintain safe operating temperatures.[IMAGE]./medtech-era-photos/medtech-10.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 10: Finished Electrical Cabinet[/CAPTION]
vlog.LearningOutcomes: The project advanced my skills from schematic-level work to enclosure-level electrical engineering. I applied clearance rules, wiring discipline, PCB design constraints, and detailed documentation practices. I learned to derive symbols and footprints from datasheets, validate substitute components, and design for maintainability. Collaborating with mechanical and software teams sharpened my skills in cross-team coordination and change management under schedule pressure.
vlog.WhatToDoDifferently: In future iterations, I would freeze component selection earlier, develop a shared symbol and footprint library before schematic capture, and perform load and thermal calculations before cabinet layout. Conducting a formal design review before PCB fabrication and cabinet drilling would reduce late-stage corrections.
vlog.FutureWorkAndWhy: Future improvements could integrate onboard current and temperature sensing, add diagnostic test points to the controller PCB, and modularize the control layer so controllers can be upgraded without rewiring the cabinet. These changes would transform the MEDTECH ERA robotic arm from a demonstration system into a reusable, academically and pre-industrially relevant robotics control platform.
